blocks.body: Balancing chemical equations ensures that the law of conservation of mass is obeyed—atoms are neither created nor destroyed during a reaction. By adjusting only the coefficients (the numbers in front of formulas) we make the number of each type of atom identical on both reactant and product sides.

items: Write the correct formulas for all reactants and products.
List the number of atoms of each element on both sides of the equation.
Start with the most complex molecule and balance elements that appear in only one reactant and one product.
Balance polyatomic ions as whole units if they appear unchanged on both sides.
Finally, balance hydrogen and oxygen, and simplify coefficients to the smallest whole-number ratio.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Only change coefficients, never subscripts. Altering subscripts creates a different compound and invalidates the reaction.

blocks.title: Balancing Chemical Equations – LibreTexts
blocks.relevance: Provides a clear, step‑by‑step guide and explains the principle of using coefficients to balance equations.

questions.q: Which of the following can be altered when balancing a chemical equation?
questions.explanation: Only coefficients (the numbers in front of formulas) may be changed; subscripts define the identity of the compound.

questions.q: In the unbalanced equation Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃, what is the smallest set of whole‑number coefficients that balances the equation?
questions.explanation: Multiplying Fe by 4, O₂ by 3, and Fe₂O₃ by 2 gives 4 Fe + 3 O₂ → 2 Fe₂O₃, which balances Fe (4 atoms) and O (6 atoms).

questions.q: When a polyatomic ion appears unchanged on both sides of the equation, the most efficient way to balance it is to:
options: Balance it as a whole unit
questions.explanation: Treating the ion as a single entity avoids unnecessary work and reduces errors.

questions.q: Why must coefficients be reduced to the smallest whole‑number ratio after balancing?
options: To match the molar masses of reactants
To reflect the simplest stoichiometric relationship
To make the equation look neat
questions.explanation: The smallest whole‑number ratio represents the true stoichiometric proportions of the reactants and products.
